one_line_takeaway: CuRD reframes IR unlearning as 'unrank-and-substitute' via a three-part knowledge-distillation loss, so a ranker can forget targeted query-document pairs without leaving tell-tale gaps, beating seven baselines on the forgetting-plus-correction trade-off while preserving retention and cutting unlearning time to ~46% of retraining.
key_finding: CuRD achieves strong forgetting AND correction simultaneously, which no single baseline does. On the Forget-10% cross-model/cross-dataset averages, Amnesiac forgets best (Pforget ~0.02 query / 0.01 document, lower is better) but corrects poorly (Pcorrect ~0.73 / 0.74) and destroys retention; Retrain/CF retain and generalise well but forget poorly (Pforget > 0.5). CuRD sits at Pforget ~0.07 query / 0.04 document while reaching the best correction (Pcorrect ~0.95 query / 0.93 document) and near-top retention (Pretain ~0.98) and generalisation (Ptest ~0.46, versus Retrain 0.47 and CF 0.49), at ~45.6% of Retrain's unlearning time. Across the appendix per-model, per-dataset tables the method (labelled 'Our' in Tables 2-3) stays in a tight band: Pforget 0.015-0.183, Pcorrect 0.863-0.993, P_delta_retain 0.018-0.037, unlearn time 0.255-2.192. The method is also parameter-insensitive (larger k modestly improves retention; gamma=0.75 degrades correction, indicating an upper limit) and converges across all forget-set sizes.
core_contribution: The paper formalises a new task, 'neural corrective machine unranking' (corrective unranking), which extends machine unlearning in neural information retrieval (IR) so that to-be-forgotten query-document pairs are not merely removed but replaced by substitute documents at the vacated ranking positions. Its own contributions are threefold: (1) a formal problem definition of corrective unranking (document removal vs. query removal, forget set F, retain set R, substitute set F*, and objective M_correct = argmin L_{S*}(w)), together with a suite of evaluation metrics for correcting (Pcorrect), forgetting (Pforget), retaining (Pretain), test/generalisation (Ptest), and an appendix metric P_delta_retain for per-sample retain drift; and (2) CuRD (Corrective unRanking Distillation), a teacher-student (knowledge-distillation) framework in which the original trained model M_train is the teacher and the student w is fine-tuned with a joint objective of three hinge-loss components: forgetting (push a forgotten pair's score down to the gamma-quantile of that query's negative-document scores), correcting (raise the substitute document's score to match the original score of the forgotten document), and retaining (keep scores of untouched positive/negative pairs unchanged). (3) Empirical demonstration that CuRD beats seven adapted baselines. Note: influence-function/gradient methods, prior distillation-unlearning (Bad Teacher, SSD, unbounded unlearning), knowledge editing, and general corrective-unlearning-for-classification are all discussed as Related Work (Section 2), not as this paper's own methods.
threat_model: This is a privacy / data-governance motivation rather than a formal attacker-vs-defender model, and the paper does not construct an active adversary. The paper's OWN security-relevant concern is that applying standard machine unlearning to an IR ranker produces observable side-channels: leaving retrieved positions empty or skipping to lower-ranked documents signals to a user/observer that content was deliberately deleted (the 'Streisand effect', exposing the unlearning action), while back-filling with the next-ranked documents can surface less-relevant or sensitive material via positional shifts (sensitive-information-leakage risk). Corrective unranking (substituting benign documents into the vacated slots) is proposed to mitigate these exposure/leakage risks while honouring deletion requests (e.g., the 'right to be forgotten'). The backdoor/poisoned-data and privacy motivations cited in Section 2 belong to GENERAL machine unlearning literature, not to this paper's threat model.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) forgetting and correcting are performed synchronously and are tightly coupled, which hinders real-time deployment where forgetting must happen immediately (e.g., a takedown request) but correction could be deferred; (2) CuRD requires explicit access to both the forget set and the retain set, limiting use where original training data is inaccessible or storage is prohibited by governance, and making it less suitable than retraining-free methods (e.g., SSD) for edge/distributed settings. Additional reviewer-visible caveats: substitute documents are randomly assigned (not semantically chosen); Ptest values are low in absolute terms (~0.4-0.6) reflecting the 1:100 hard-negative setup; the main-text Forget-10% numbers are aggregated ranges/averages rather than significance-tested; and the convergence/stability 'guarantees' are informal (convexity in the relevance scores plus mild-condition arguments), not full proofs.

datasets_used: MS MARCO (passage ranking; adapted to ~1:100 positive:negative per query)
TREC CAR (adapted to ~1:100 positive:negative per query)
quantitative_results: CuRD Forget-10% avg across 4 models/2 datasets: Pforget ~0.07 (query removal), ~0.04 (document removal) [lower is better]
Amnesiac Forget-10% avg: Pforget ~0.02 (query) / ~0.01 (document) but Pcorrect only ~0.73 (query) / ~0.74 (document)
CuRD Forget-10% avg correction: Pcorrect ~0.95 (query removal), ~0.93 (document removal) [higher is better], exceeding Retrain and CF
CuRD Forget-10% avg retention Pretain ~0.98; generalisation Ptest ~0.46 (vs Retrain 0.47, CF 0.49)
CuRD unlearning time ~45.6% of Retrain's on average
CuRD overall per-model/per-dataset ranges (appendix Tables 2-3, 'Our' rows): Pforget 0.015-0.183, Pcorrect 0.863-0.993, P_delta_retain 0.018-0.037, unlearn time 0.255-2.192
MS MARCO per-model (CuRD/'Our') Pforget query removal: BERTcat 0.022, BERTdot 0.020, ColBERT 0.026, PARADE 0.015
MS MARCO per-model (CuRD/'Our') Pcorrect query removal: BERTcat 0.957, BERTdot 0.987, ColBERT 0.955, PARADE 0.964
Parameter sensitivity: k=5 gives Pretain 0.978; k=10/15 give Pretain ~0.990; gamma=0.75 raises Pforget (query) to 0.075 and drops Pcorrect (query) to 0.849, indicating an optimal upper bound on gamma

Amnesiac and SSD catastrophically damage retention (Pretain dropping below 0.1 for Amnesiac; SSD Pcorrect below ~0.35 on average), while NegGrad/BadT show moderate retain-set degradation (P_delta_retain ~0.144-0.151 avg)
baselines_compared: Retrain (retrain from scratch on modified retain set S*)
Catastrophic Forgetting / CF (continue training M_train on S*)
Negative Gradient / NegGrad (reverse-gradient unlearning then fine-tune on substitutes)
Bad Teacher / BadT (random-init bad teacher on forget samples, competent teacher on S*)
Selective Synaptic Dampening / SSD (importance-based parameter editing)
Contrastive and Consistent Loss / CoCoL (distillation-based unlearning tailored to neural IR)
